english: ing about the conversion of others; ye-śes-la (ye shes la) to understand all writings on religion (ni f.); čos-la (chos la) to convey the publication of religion to all creatures at the same time and in every language. 3. in practical mysticism: various supernatural powers (v. skur-ba (skur ba)), e.g. pyi naṅ gsaṅ-gi dbaṅ skur-ba (phyi nang gsang gi dbang skur ba) Pth. is alledged to signify: to convey externally, i.e. into the mouth, the power of snaṅ-ba-mta-yás (snang ba mtha' yas) (this and the two following are names of Buddhas and demons), internally, into the body, the power of spyan-ras-gzigs (spyan ras gzigs) and lastly into the mind perfect purity, i.e. the rta-mgrin (rta mgrin), and together with it power over the demons. — 3. regard, consideration(?). In later writings the composition of dbaṅ-du byás-na (dbang du byas na) (mdzád-na (mdzad na) etc.) c. genit. (instead of which in C. also dbaṅ-du śór-na (dbang du shor na), soṅ-na (song na) are said to be in use), is frq. to be met with, signifying as much as: when ... is concerned, when ... is in question, for the purpose of, or merely: respecting, as regards: légs-pai (legs pa'i), ǰigs-pai ('jigs pa'i), btsán-pai (btsan pa'i) when beauty, firmness, formidable appearance (of a royal castle) are concerned, are the points in question Glr.; sṅags-kyi dbaṅ-du rtsis-pai śló-ka (sngags kyi dbang du rtsis pa'i shlo ka) prob. the Slokas being numbered with a regard to the Mantras, i.e. including the latter Tar. 127, 16. — 4. symb. num.: 5 (dbaṅ (dbang) being taken for dbaṅ-po (dbang po)). Comp. and deriv. dbaṅ-bskúr (dbang bskur) consecration, inauguration, initiation Was. (189), = dbaṅ-bskyúr (dbang bskyur) might, power, e.g. saṅs-rgyás kyi (sangs rgyas kyi) Glr. — dbaṅ-grál (dbang gral) the row of those that are to be ordained or consecrated. — dbaṅ-čan (dbang can) mighty, powerful Cs. — dbaṅ-táṅ (dbang thang) 1. might, = mṅa-táṅ (mnga' thang), dbaṅ-taṅ-méd-pa (dbang thang med pa) low, mean, of inferior rank Dzl. 2. time, chronology Lexx. 3. destiny, fate, predestined fate, or rather the destiny of any creature consequent to its former actions, tse daṅ dbaṅ-táṅ (tshe dang dbang thang), frq.; dbaṅ-taṅ-méd-pa (dbang thang med pa) may therefore imply: having no destiny, i.e. no particular destiny. — dbaṅ-dus-po-braṅ (dbang dus po brang) 'Angdophorung' of the Indian papers, n. of a fort in Tibet. — dbaṅ-ldán (dbang ldan) mighty, powerful; dbaṅ-ldán-gyi pyogs (dbang ldan gyi phyogs) Domaṅ, dbaṅ-poi pyogs (dbang po'i phyogs) Sbh., is said to be north-east. — dbaṅ-po (dbang po) v. the next article. — dbaṅ-pyúg (dbang phyug) 1. adj. mighty, also sbst.: dbaṅ-pyúg gzáṅ-las čé-ba (dbang phyug gzang las che ba) Glr. 2. symb. num.: 11. 3. noun proper a. Iswara, Siva Glr., hence also the Lingam as his emblem Glr. b. Avalokitesvara Glr. — dbáṅ-ba (dbang ba) 1. vb. c. la = dbaṅ byéd-pa (dbang byed pa), e.g. rgyal-srid-la mi dbáṅ (rgyal srid la mi dbang) he does not succeed to the throne; gen. with accus. gčig ḍi dbáṅ-ba yin (gcig 'di dbang ba yin) one ... belongs to this one Mil.; bdag dbáṅ-bai rgyal-prán (bdag dbang ba'i rgyal phran) the vassals under my sway Dzl. 2. sbst. = dbaṅ (dbang), e.g. tsé-la dbáṅ-ba (tshe la dbang ba) (v. above). — dbaṅ-rís (dbang ris) prob. domain, dominion. — dbaṅ(-po)-lág(-pa) (dbang (-po) lag (-pa)) a medicine, said to be prepared from a viscid aromatic root, shaped like a hand. — dbaṅ-śés (dbang shes) perception, by means of the organs of sense Was. (278).

1. possessed of power, dominion, nor-gyi dbáṅ-por gyur (nor gyi dbang por gyur) Dzl. (Ms.); lord, ruler, sovereign, esp. divine rulers: Indra, also lhaí dbáṅ-po (lha'i dbang po); further rgyál-bai dbáṅ-po (rgyal ba'i dbang po), túb-pai dbáṅ-po (thub pa'i dbang po) the highest of the Buddhas Glr. — 2. organ of sense, dbáṅ-po lṅa(-po) (dbang po lnga (-po)) a. the five organs of sense, eyes etc., also dbáṅ-poi sgo lṅa (dbang po'i sgo lnga) Med. b. Trigl. 17, 6, five immaterial, transcendent senses of Buddha, which are in unison with his five powers, stobs lṅa (stobs lnga), as stated by Burn. II, 430, v. Köpp. I, 436. In natural philosophy six organs of sense frq. are mentioned, मनस being added as the sixth; medical writings also treat of dbaṅ-po dgu (dbang po dgu) or dbáṅ-poi sgo dgu (dbang po'i sgo dgu), v. bú-ga (bu ga). — 3. sense, intellectual power, dbáṅ-po rnón-po (dbang po rnon po) of acute intellect, dbáṅ-po rdúl-po (dbang po rdul po) of obtuse intellect, also as common expressions for sagacious or dull Dzl.; dbáṅ-po nyams (dbang po nyams) the senses are weakened, become dull Med.; lus sems dbáṅ-po (lus sems dbang po) body, soul, and senses (are glad, are pure etc.) Dom.; dbáṅ-po gsó-ba (dbang po gso ba) to gladden, strengthen, revive, the senses Mil.; raṅ-gi séms-las dbáṅ-poi rnam-śés ˚byuṅ (rang gi sems las dbang po'i rnam shes 'byung) out of the spirit (of the personality which during the time between two periods of existence is in a disembodied state) the sense-endowed soul (of the new individual) is generated (in the process of conception)
